faq.body: Podable provides objective play metrics for Commander Pods. It does this by tracking gameplay data (like life totals, commander damage, poison counters, turn durations, and bracket ratings) then ties it to Commanders to build up averages of: win rates, game speeds, and bracket distributions of your local meta. Players can also share their Pod data to build up global leaderboards of the top performing Commanders around the world!

Podable keeps track of the game state though user input. The Pods are the main way to interact during game play. When damage occurs in game, use the damage buttons to change a players life total. The color of the buttons will change before applying commander damage.
Pods are the circles in each player's quadrant. Tap them to show or hide damage buttons and reset damage type.

• Opponent quadrants: Deal commander damage to them
• Title bar 'TAX' side: Add commander tax
• StarPod: Create the BombPod!
Double tap the StarPod to advance turns. It will rotate to face the active player and their space will be highlighted. The StarPod shows the turn number.
Drag the BombPod into a quadrant to declare a special elimination condition.  Double tap the BombPod to deal 1 damage to everyone else.
Tap the Log Bar to input Commanders. Type the first few letters and choose from suggestions.
Partner Commanders prompt you to input both. 'Partner with' pairs are automatically suggested. During games, tap the Log Bar to switch which partner is active.

Drag your Pod on to the Log Bar's 'TAX' side when casting your Commander. Drag on to the opposite side to remove accidentally placed tax.  Tax is added to the active Commander (so be careful when using partners).
Drag the Star Pod into your quadrant to declare alternate wins. A menu will appear with win condition options.
Track poison counters with the Φ symbol (green + to add, red − to remove). For non damage eliminations, drag the BombPod to your quadrant and select 'Concede' or 'Milled'.

faq.subtitle: It's not if you win or lose, it's how you play the game

faq.body: Winning feels great, but remember three other people just lost. Avoid gloating and instead highlight opponents' clever strategies. Be the player who makes games fun for everyone.

faq.subtitle: The game ends with a handshake

faq.body: Defeated players should offer handshakes to the winner. This shows good sportsmanship and acknowledges the outcome. Games without handshakes are considered contentious.

Podable's insights can give you advantages, but the goal remains fun, balanced games. Use data to improve your play and build better decks, not to dominate casual tables. Share interesting patterns with your playgroup since everyone benefits from a more engaging meta.

"On the turn the Sol is Cast,
'Round the arrow a Pod must pass,
Circle thrice to log the turn
Expose the Ring which wizards yearn."

All game data stays private to your account unless you choose to contribute anonymized statistics to community insights. Games which are synced to the cloud via YeetPods (our data sync service), builds a database of commander performance, bracket accuracy, and meta insights that improve over time.

about.body: Podable provides objective play metrics for Commander Pods. It does this by tracking gameplay data (like life totals, commander damage, poison counters, turn durations, and bracket ratings) then ties it to Commanders to build up averages of: win rates, game speeds, and bracket distributions of your local meta. Players can also share their Pod data to build up global leaderboards of the top performing Commanders around the world!
Podable aims to never include advertisements within the platform and keep access to data it receives by user submissions freely available to Podable users.

Logged Pods which were created from games IRL can always be Yeeted to the cloud via YeetPods. PodPasses are items that get attached to selected Yeeted Pod data. These are meant to be symbolzers which tell the server that the data it receives is coming from a reliable source and to put less scrutany on the verification of the Pod data. By keeping Pod Logs as close to the IRL game as possible, the community gives their thanks to your dedication towards our hobby and for making it better for everyone.

Players who continually Yeet their Pods to the cloud may eventually unlock the ability to become a Content Creator for Podable News.  The News feature is a way for players to keep current with new or novel ideas as the franchise of Magic: the Gathering evolves. Writing articles for the News outlet gives players a platform to express the ideas which they feel are important enough to share with the community at large. Players who are offered an invite to the News Creator Badge will become part of the News Moderation team and screen new articles prior to their release.  There is no obigation for Badge holders to write News articles, however, they will be required to rate News articles each month in order to keep their moderation status.  Players who submit or approve of News stories which contain topics outside of MTG and/or contain advertisements will be removed from the program. The community is built of wonderful people and we hope to read their stories soon!
Syncs Pods to the PodDB cloud server database to calculate global statistics.

Foxcoon Industries Inc.


contact@foxcoon-industries.ca

©️ 2025

secrets.body: Commander has social rules that determine who gets invited back to Pods. These govern everything from threat assessment to victory celebrations. Podable's data helps you navigate these unwritten social dynamics.

Rule Zero lets players modify restrictions through group discussion, but it's often used to justify bringing powerful cards to casual tables. The bracket system was designed to replace these negotiations.

brackets.body: Wizards of the Coast recently released the bracket system for Magic: The Gathering commander players to assign their decks into. The goal was to provide a categorical framework for deck selection based on various limitations (such as a set number of powerful cards) and to encourage players to start on equal footing with each other.

What the company has failed to communicate is that this issue isn't about the power level of the decks themselves, but rather about the intent of the people playing them. The most important part of which bracket a deck falls into is HOW the decks' pilot intends to use the deck in a Pod. The brackets should NOT be thought of as a scale from low power to high power, but as distinctly separate categories of play.

Podable is an app for commander players, built by commander players. As such, we want to accurately relay (for better or for worse) the ways actual ways Magic players are using the bracket system, as well as various terms and phrases one might over hear at Friday Night Magic.  Here, we discuss a more intuitive way on how to think of brackets and why player intent ultimately determines which bracket to assign a deck into.

Bracket 1 focuses on thematic world-building through the thoughtful choice of cards.  Magic: The Gathering is a game built on a plethora of fantasy worlds, each with extensive backstories. Players are incentivized to weave a coherent story with each card they play. This bracket is commonly misinterpreted as being for weak cards and weak decks. This is untrue. Powerful decks can be built from thematic card sets; it just takes creativity to unlock that power. In Bracket 1, players focus on sharing an experience rather than trying to win at all costs.

Bracket 2 is for players who wish to test out their deck-building and piloting skills. Decks in this bracket are usually ones made from a new pile or have typically undergone several revisions since the last played game. Players should be eager to get their deck to "do the thing."  Whether thats trying to complete a new unseen combo or testing out a novel synergy between pet cards, this bracket aims to value exploration of strategy over well known cases. If everyone in the Pod gets a chance to do "the thing", the game is considered a success.

Bracket 3 seems to be the default choice for anyone who just wants to play the game. Here is where players begin to focus their aim towards winning the game. Decks with varied gameplay and strategy emerge as a result of players trying to one-up each other during the Pod. A set number of "Game Changer" cards can be included within the deck, but these are not a requirement to participate and enjoy playing in this bracket. 
 Note: Modified and preconstructed decks form the basis of Bracket 3. The term "Precon" (preconstructed) is a widely used term in the community, and as such, should be explicitly redefined to better reflect how players use it in practice.
"Precon": A pre-game con pulled on opponents regarding a decks' power level, where a player tries to convince the table that the deck they purchased is weaker than it actually is; usually by claiming they only swapped out <number> of cards.

Bracket 4 is for players who haven't yet realized that dying in a game does NOT mean they die in real life. As a result, this bracket is for players who keep grudges against others and are unlikely have developed the ability to lose with grace.  Players in this bracket often build decks which prioritize frustrating, uninspiring, and/or repetitive play patterns. Imposing mental anguish on their friends is often a goal for Bracket 4 players.  Decks played in this bracket are highly tuned and packed with many powerful game-changing cards. These decks are unfortunately often fairly predictable (usually from being net-decked), where knowing the commander is usually enough to guess the expected strategy or types of cards likely to be included.

Bracket 5 focuses on the most strategy of all: winning. Games typically revolve around politics/deal-making and usually last for only a few turns. Very little variation exists in the construction of decks within this bracket. This bracket is more commonly known as cEDH (Competitive Commander).  Players homogenize their decks to cheat out a win as fast as possible using the most broken cards ever printed over all of Magic's history. Since many of the best cards come from an era when Magic's designers weren't thinking about multiplayer, these powerful cards are often extremely expensive. As a result, players fill their decks with proxy (counterfeit) cards and pretend they're the real deal. Podable takes no ethical stance on proxy usage, rather just acknowledges that "it is what it is."

Magic: The Gathering players have various reasons for including certain power cards, which can range from exploring new combos to reusing powerful cards in hopes of sneaking in a win. Game changers are a set of cards which Wizards of the Coast have acknowledged their ability to warp games around them. In practice, this means that the fundamental way games are played changes once one or more of these are on the table. Players will abandon their decks unique strategy as these cards become the main focus of the table. Players shift their decisions to play around them, try to acquire them for their use, or become stalled out and unable to play because of them. Podable encourages players to take mental note of when they see cards which cause similar game refocusing effects in their Pods, as knowing which cards need to added to this list might be helpful for to company to know about!

Podable can keep track of bracket ratings and analyzes their relative accuracy by letting opponents rate the other decks in the Pod after the game finishes. This bracket disparity can be seen with the Vibe Check chart found in the Brackets Statistics page. While Wizards of the Coast has set forth a good first step, the real solution is probably comes from the use of data-driven insights about actual deck performances.

Players assign the bracket the feel their deck falls into during Pod setup. After the match, players rate each other's decks and assign the bracket they felt their opponents actually tried to play in. This dual Vibes rating aims to reduces bias by highlighting whether your bracket choice matches reality. 

When a player wins a game, the bracket they chose becomes the ground truth for their deck. This implies that game winner's are more in tune with the level at which they claim to play at. However, opponents also get a say in the bracket ratings each other's decks. This bracket accuracy is measured as a percentage of the number of opponents who assign the same bracket to the winners deck as the winner did. This secondary accuracy metric helps players gauge whether their initial rating was fair. If a player notices that opponents consistently rate their deck differently, it may be worth reflecting on why and possibly choosing a different bracket for next time.

analytics.subtitle: Unlock the Hidden Patterns in Your Games

analytics.body: Podable's advanced analytics transform raw game data into actionable strategic insights. Each visualization reveals different aspects of your gameplay, helping you understand not just what happened, but why it happened and how to improve.

Traditional tracking tells you what happened. Podable's analytics tell you WHY it happened and HOW to improve. The Game Flow Heat Map, for example, shows exactly when games are won and lost, helping you time your plays perfectly.
Insights improve with more data. With just a few games per commander, basic patterns emerge. With as the database of games grows, the analytics become more reliable for strategic planning.

analytics.title: How do I improve using these tools?

analytics.body: Each tool has a specific use case for understanding they way you play.

The Performance overview will give you an idea of which decks have high potential. Commanders which have high win rates but low play rate might have enough game data yet to correlate between wins and losses. The more you play, the more accurate the statistics become.

Use the Game Flow Heat Map to analyse individual games and identify common turn patterns.  If the heat maps show you being eliminated during high-intensity periods, you may need better defensive options or threat assessment skills. Predicting when opponents tend to be aggressive can give you an reactionary advantage when you're considering holding onto removal or a counter.
Use the Ecosystem Map to understand favourable matchups for deciding which decks to play. Picking the right deck for the Pod can be crucial for securing the win.

foxcoon-industries.ca.body: Foxcoon Industries Inc. is a world class company with top tier programmers. We always love what we do and put ourselves our work. Our product, Podable, is developed through hours of reseach playing paper games with all kinds of people in order to find the most optimum way to record games and minimizes the resistance from players.
